course.title: Clio: Your Terminal Assistant
course.description: Learn to use Clio — the offline-first CLI assistant that turns plain English into shell commands. Built for Termux and Nigerian students who want to code on their phone without memorizing Linux syntax.
course.objectives: Install Clio, ask questions in plain English and Pidgin, run setup wizards, download automation modules, and work confidently on Termux.
course.prerequisites: Basic familiarity with opening a terminal (Termux or Linux).

lessons.title: Lesson 1 — What Is Clio?
lessons.description: Install Clio and understand what it does on your phone or laptop.
lessons.content:
# Lesson 1 — What Is Clio?

Welcome, future digital wizards! Are you ready to unlock the true power of your phone or computer? If you've ever felt intimidated by the "black screen" of the terminal, or found yourself struggling to remember complex commands, then you're in the perfect place. This micro-course is designed to transform your terminal experience, making it intuitive, powerful, and fun, especially for those of you on Android phones using Termux.

In this first lesson, we'll introduce you to **Clio**, your new best friend in the terminal. We'll cover what Clio is, why it's a game-changer for students like you, how to get it installed, and how to have your very first conversation with it.

---

## The Terminal on Your Phone: A Challenge and an Opportunity

Imagine trying to type a long, complex sentence on a tiny phone keyboard, in a language you're still learning. That's often what using a traditional terminal feels like for many students, especially on a mobile device. Commands like `ls -lart` or `find . -name "*.txt" -exec rm {} \;` can be daunting to memorize and even harder to type correctly without a full keyboard.

But the terminal is also where the real power lies – it's where you can automate tasks, manage files like a pro, and dive deep into development. What if there was a way to bridge that gap? What if you could just tell your phone what you wanted to do, in plain language, and it understood?

That's exactly what Clio does.

## What is Clio, Really? Your Natural Language Assistant

At its heart, **Clio is your offline-first command-line interface (CLI) assistant**. Think of it as a smart translator between your everyday language and the technical commands your computer understands. Instead of memorizing arcane syntax, you simply type what you want to achieve, and Clio suggests the correct shell command.

For example, instead of remembering `du -sh *` to check disk space, you can simply ask Clio:

```
>> check disk space
```

Clio will then present you with the appropriate command and ask for your confirmation before running it. This means you're always in control, and you learn the commands as you go!

## Why Clio is Your Best Friend on Termux (and Beyond)

Clio wasn't just built for any terminal user; it was specifically designed with students like you in mind, especially those who rely on Termux on their Android phones. Here's why Clio stands out:

1.  **Offline-First Magic**: Imagine you're in a place with no internet, or your data plan is exhausted. Many online tools become useless. Clio, however, works perfectly fine offline for most common tasks. It has a built-in "brain" (its phrase and catalog layers) that understands and matches your requests to commands without needing to connect to the internet. This is a huge advantage for learning and productivity anywhere, anytime.

2.  **Understands Nigerian Pidgin**: Yes, you read that right! Clio is smart enough to understand common Nigerian Pidgin phrases. This makes interacting with your terminal feel much more natural and less like you're speaking to a robot. So, feel free to say things like "abeg show me files for downloads folder" or "wetin dey my current folder."

3.  **Built for Termux & Low RAM Devices**: Clio is designed to be lightweight and efficient. It runs smoothly on Termux on Android phones, even those with limited RAM. It won't slow down your device or crash unexpectedly, ensuring a stable and productive experience. This "Lite profile" means you get powerful features without sacrificing performance.

4.  **You Stay in Control (Confirm Before Run)**: A common fear for beginners is accidentally running a destructive command. Clio eliminates this worry. Before executing *any* shell command, Clio will always show you what it plans to do and ask for your permission. This is your safety net, giving you the chance to review, learn, or cancel.

5.  **Smart Categorization**: When Clio suggests a command, it clearly tells you what kind of suggestion it is. You'll see headers like `SHELL COMMAND`, `AUTOMATION MODULE`, or `SETUP WIZARD`. This helps you understand the context and power of the suggestion.

Here's a quick comparison to highlight Clio's advantages:

| Feature                   | Traditional Terminal (e.g., Termux) | Clio on Termux lessons.title: Lesson 2 — Ask in Plain English
lessons.description: Use natural language, Pidgin, and full sentences to find shell commands.
lessons.content:
# Lesson 2 — Ask in Plain English

Welcome back, future terminal masters! In our last lesson, we got Clio installed and took a quick peek at its power. Today, we're diving into the heart of what makes Clio so special: its ability to understand *you*. Forget memorizing complex commands; with Clio, you just ask.

### Why Asking in Plain English Matters (Especially on Your Phone!)

Imagine you're on your Android phone, using Termux. Typing long, cryptic commands like `grep -rnw '/path/to/somewhere/' -e 'pattern'` on a small screen can be a real headache. It's easy to make typos, and remembering all those flags (`-r`, `-n`, `-w`) is tough, especially when you're just starting out.

This is where Clio shines! It's built for folks like you, who might be new to the Linux terminal or simply want a faster, more intuitive way to get things done. Clio lets you use the language you're comfortable with – plain English, or even Nigerian Pidgin – to get the exact shell command you need. No more struggling with syntax; just tell Clio what you want to achieve.

---

## Your Intent, Not Just Syntax

Clio is an **offline-first CLI assistant**, meaning it has a vast catalog of commands ready even without an internet connection. It doesn't just look for keywords; it tries to understand your *intent*. This means you don't need to know the exact command; you just describe the task.

### Instant Shell Commands from Everyday Language

Think of Clio as your personal dictionary for the terminal. You describe a task, and it suggests the most relevant shell command. These are the commands you'd typically run directly in your Termux or Linux terminal.

Let's look at some common tasks and how Clio translates them:

| You Type (in Clio's `>>` prompt) | Clio Suggests (e.g., in "Run the command" option) | What it Does |
| :-------------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------ | :----------- |
| `list files`                      | `ls`                                              | Shows files and folders in your current directory. |
| `check disk space`                | `df -h`                                           | Displays free and used disk space in a human-readable format. |
| `find large files`                | `find . -type f -size +100M -print0 \| xargs -0 du -h` | Locates files larger than 100MB in your current directory. |
| `copy file source.txt destination.txt` | `cp source.txt destination.txt`                   | Copies `source.txt` to `destination.txt`. |
| `check memory`                    | `free -h`                                         | Shows how much memory is free and used. |
| `show processes`                  | `ps aux`                                          | Lists all running processes. |
| `remove empty folders`            | `find . -type d -empty -delete`                   | Deletes empty directories recursively. |

**How it helps:** Instead of remembering `df -h` or `find . -type f -size +100M`, you just say what you want. Clio does the heavy lifting of recalling the precise command.

---

## Full Sentences Work Like Magic

You don't need to speak in keywords. Clio is smart enough to understand full, natural sentences. This makes interacting with your terminal much more conversational and less like programming.

```bash
>> how do I see which processes are running
```
*(Clio will likely suggest `ps aux` or similar)*

```bash
>> I want to extract a zip file named my_archive.zip
```
*(Clio will likely suggest `unzip my_archive.zip`)*

```bash
>> show me files modified in the last week in my documents folder
```
*(Clio will likely suggest something like `find ~/storage/shared/Documents -type f -mtime -7`)*

```bash
>> what is my public IP address
```
*(Clio will likely suggest a command that uses `curl` or `dig` to an external service)*

This capability is fantastic for beginners because it bridges the gap between your thought process and the terminal's requirements. You think "I want to do X," and you can literally type that.

---

## Nigerian Pidgin & Campus Talk: Clio Understands You!

Clio is built with *you* in mind. We know that English isn't always the most comfortable language for everyone, and casual phrasing is common. That's why Clio has been trained to understand Nigerian Pidgin and common campus slang. This makes it even easier and more natural to get help.

Imagine you're chatting with a friend about a terminal problem; you can use similar language with Clio!

```bash
>> wetin dey inside this folder
```
*(Clio will understand this as "what is inside this folder?" and suggest `ls`)*

```bash
>> abeg help me check memory
```
*(Clio will understand "please help me check memory" and suggest `free -h`)*

```bash
>> I wan install python for termux
```
*(Clio will understand "I want to install Python for Termux" and likely suggest `pkg install python` or guide you towards a **SETUP WIZARD** for Python, which we'll cover in Lesson 3!)*

```bash
>> how I go delete this file wey no dey useful again
```
*(Clio will understand "how do I delete this file that is no longer useful?" and suggest `rm <filename>`)*

```bash
>> my phone storage full, wetin I fit do
```
*(Clio might suggest `df -h` or guide you to `find` large files, showing you practical steps to free up space)*

This feature makes Clio incredibly accessible and user-friendly, especially for students on Termux who are just getting started with command-line tools. It removes a significant language barrier, allowing you to focus on learning and doing, not on translating your thoughts into rigid commands.

---

## The Result Menu: Your Options After a Match

When Clio finds a match for your query, it doesn't just spit out a command. It gives you a helpful menu of options to ensure you understand and control what happens next. This is crucial for safety and learning.

Let's say you typed `>> how do I check my internet connection`. Clio processes this and presents a menu like this:

```
⌨️ SHELL COMMAND: Check network connectivity

1. Show examples
2. Run the command: ping -c 4 google.com
3. Search again
4. Exit
```

Here's what each option means:

1.  **Show examples**: This is your learning companion! Selecting this will display common flags, alternative uses, and explanations for the suggested command (`ping` in this case). It's an excellent way to deepen your understanding without leaving Clio.
2.  **Run the command**: This option will execute the suggested shell command directly in your terminal. **Clio will always ask for your confirmation before running any shell command.** This is a critical safety feature. Always read the command carefully before typing `y` for yes!
3.  **Search again**: If the suggested command isn't quite what you wanted, or you want to refine your query, choose this to go back and try a different phrasing.
4.  **Exit**: Leaves the current Clio interaction and returns you to your regular terminal prompt.

---

## Understanding Match Types (Look at the Header!)

Clio doesn't just find commands; it finds *solutions*. These solutions come in different "types," and Clio clearly labels them in the header of the result menu. This helps you understand what kind of action Clio is proposing.

When you see a match, pay attention to the header:

*   **⌨️ SHELL COMMAND**:
    *   **What it is:** This is a standard Linux/Termux command that will run directly in your terminal. Most of what we've discussed so far falls into this category.
    *   **Example:** `ls`, `df -h`, `cp`, `ping`.
    *   **Action:** Clio will offer to `Run the command` after confirmation.
    *   **Think:** "This is a single command I can execute now."

*   **📦 AUTOMATION MODULE**:
    *   **What it is:** This indicates that Clio has found a more complex, repeatable workflow or script from the CLIPilot registry. These modules are not bundled with Clio; they are downloaded when you need them and can automate multi-step tasks.
    *   **Example:** You might ask `>> setup a web server` or `>> backup my contacts`.
    *   **Action:** Clio will first offer to `Download the module` (if you don't have it) and then `Run the module`. (We'll dive deep into Automation Modules in **Lesson 4**).
    *   **Think:** "This is a smart script that can do a bigger task for me."

*   **⭐ SETUP WIZARD**:
    *   **What it is:** These are special, guided workflows designed to help you install, configure, or set up popular tools and environments like Python, Git, Vim, or even Termux itself. They simplify complex installation processes.
    *   **Example:** You might ask `>> install git` or `>> configure vim`.
    *   **Action:** Clio will offer to `Run the wizard`, which will guide you through a series of interactive steps. (We'll explore Setup Wizards extensively in **Lesson 3**).
    *   **Think:** "This will guide me step-by-step to install or set up something important."

Understanding these headers is key to knowing what Clio is about to do and how to proceed.

---

## Try It Now: Practice Asking Clio!

It's time to get your hands dirty! Open Termux, start Clio by typing `clio`, and try asking it some questions. Remember, the prompt is `>>`.

**Your Mission:**

1.  **Ask five different questions** to Clio.
2.  **Mix your phrasing:** Use formal English, full sentences, and Nigerian Pidgin.
3.  **Observe the Match Type:** Note whether Clio suggests a `⌨️ SHELL COMMAND`, `📦 AUTOMATION MODULE`, or `⭐ SETUP WIZARD`.
4.  **Explore "Show examples":** For at least two `SHELL COMMAND` suggestions, choose "Show examples" to see what extra information Clio provides.
5.  **Always confirm:** If you choose to "Run the command," carefully read what Clio suggests and only confirm if you understand and trust it.

Here are some ideas to get you started:

*   "abeg, wetin dey inside this folder?"
*   "how to check my remaining storage?"
*   "I wan install python"
*   "show me processes that are using a lot of memory"
*   "how do I create a new directory?"
*   "my network no dey work, how I go check am?"
*   "what is my username?"

**Self-Reflection:**
*   Was it easy to get Clio to understand you?
*   Did you find the "Show examples" option useful?
*   Did you notice the different match types?

---

## Common Mistakes & Troubleshooting

Even with natural language, sometimes Clio might not give you exactly what you expect. Don't worry, it's part of the learning process!

*   **Too Vague:** If you just type `files`, Clio might not know *what* you want to do with files. Try to be a bit more specific: `list files`, `delete a file`, `find a file`.
*   **Typo Troubles:** While Clio is forgiving, major typos can confuse it. Double-check your spelling if Clio gives an unexpected result.
*   **Context Missing:** Clio understands many things, but it can't read your mind. If you're talking about a specific file, include its name in your query (e.g., `delete my_report.txt`).
*   **"No match found":** If Clio can't find anything, it means your query was too obscure or it simply doesn't have that specific knowledge yet. Try rephrasing your question in a different way.

---

## Lesson 2 Checklist

You've successfully completed Lesson 2 when you can confidently say "yes" to these points:

*   I understand that Clio matches my *intent*, not just exact command syntax.
*   I can use plain English and full sentences to ask Clio for shell commands.
*   I know that Clio understands Nigerian Pidgin and casual phrasing.
*   I can identify the `>>` prompt for interacting with Clio.
*   I understand the options in Clio's result menu: "Show examples," "Run the command," and "Search again."
*   I know that Clio **always asks for confirmation** before running a shell command.
*   I can distinguish between `⌨️ SHELL COMMAND`, `📦 AUTOMATION MODULE`, and `⭐ SETUP WIZARD` by looking at the header.
*   I have practiced asking Clio at least five questions and observed the match types.

---

Great job, everyone! You're now equipped to speak to your terminal assistant in a natural, intuitive way. In our next lesson, **Lesson 3: Setting Up Your Tools**, we'll explore how Clio's powerful `⭐ SETUP WIZARDS` can simplify installing and configuring essential tools for your development journey. Keep practicing, and see you there!

lessons.title: Lesson 3 — Setup Wizards
lessons.description: Use first-class setup wizards to configure Termux, Vim, Git, languages, and databases.
lessons.content:
# Lesson 3 — Setup Wizards

"Abeg, setting up my phone for coding na wahala!" If this sounds like you, then today's lesson is your answer! We know how challenging it can be to transform your Android phone into a powerful development workstation using Termux. From installing the right tools to configuring them correctly, it often feels like you need to be a guru just to get started.

That's where Clio's **Setup Wizards** come in. Imagine having a smart assistant that knows exactly what you need to get your environment ready, whether it's setting up Git, installing Python, or configuring Vim with all the cool plugins. Clio's Setup Wizards are designed to handle these complex initial configurations for you, making your journey into coding much smoother and less frustrating. They are your first-class ticket to a well-prepared development environment, right from your phone.

## What Are Setup Wizards?

Think of **Setup Wizards** as Clio's specialized tools for **installing and configuring your development environment**. They are designed to handle tasks you usually do *once* when you're setting up a new project or a new machine (in our case, your Termux environment on your phone).

They are super helpful because they:
*   **Save you time:** No more searching online for endless commands and troubleshooting obscure errors.
*   **Prevent mistakes:** Wizards follow best practices, reducing the chances of misconfigurations.
*   **Provide consistency:** Ensure your setup is robust and ready for development.

It's important to remember that Setup Wizards are **not** the same as Automation Modules (which we'll explore in Lesson 4). While both automate tasks, wizards are for *initial setup*, while modules are for *repeatable tasks* within your workflow.

## How to Access Setup Wizards

Clio makes it simple to find and run these powerful wizards. You have a few ways to get started:

### 1. List All Wizards

If you're not sure which wizard you need, or you just want to see what's available, simply type `setup` at the Clio prompt:

```
>> setup
```

Clio will then list all the available setup wizards, giving you an overview of what each one can do. This is a great starting point for exploration!

### 2. Run a Wizard Directly

If you already know which wizard you want to use, you can call it by name. For example, to set up your Termux environment:

```
>> setup termux
```

Similarly, you can directly invoke others:

```
>> setup vim
>> setup git
>> setup devtools
>> setup database
```

### 3. Use Natural Language

Clio understands natural language, including a bit of Nigerian Pidgin! This means you don't always have to remember the exact command. You can just tell Clio what you want to do:

```
>> configure my dev environment
```

```
>> install git and gh
```

```
>> setup vim plugins
```

```
>> abeg, I wan configure Termux wey go make sense
```

When Clio identifies that your request matches a Setup Wizard, it will clearly indicate this with a special label in the output:

**⭐ [SETUP WIZARD]**

This label is your confirmation that Clio is about to run a dedicated wizard designed for your task, not just a simple shell command.

## Available Wizards: Your Toolkit for Termux

Let's take a closer look at the powerful setup wizards Clio offers and what each one can help you achieve. This table expands on the draft to give you more context for each wizard.

| Wizard                   | Purpose & What it Does

lessons.title: Lesson 4 — Automation Modules
lessons.description: Download and run repeatable task workflows from the CLIPilot registry.
lessons.content:
# Lesson 4 — Automation Modules

**Lesson summary:** Download and run repeatable task workflows from the CLIPilot registry.
**Target duration:** 12 minutes

Wetin dey, my people? For our last lesson, we learned how Clio helps us set up our Termux environment with **Setup Wizards**. But what about the things we do *every day*? Copying files, checking storage, backing up important notes, installing specific tools for a project... these small, repeatable tasks can be tedious, especially when you're typing on a small phone screen.

Imagine you could just tell Clio, "Abeg, check my disk space," or "I wan backup this folder," and it just *does it*. That's exactly what **Automation Modules** are for! They are like ready-made scripts that Clio can download and run for you, saving you time and stress. No need to remember complex commands or type them out repeatedly. This lesson will show you how to find, download, and use these powerful tools.

## What are Automation Modules?

Think of **Automation Modules** as smart shortcuts for common, repeatable tasks. They are pre-written workflows designed to handle specific jobs. Instead of typing a long series of commands every time you want to, say, backup your project, you can use one simple module.

**Examples of what modules can do:**
*   **File management:** Copying files, moving directories, creating backups.
*   **System checks:** Checking available disk space, monitoring running processes.
*   **Development tasks:** Installing specific language packages, setting up boilerplate project structures.
*   **Network tasks:** Checking network connectivity, downloading files from a specific source.

These modules are not bundled with Clio's main binary. Instead, they are downloaded from the **CLIPilot registry** as you need them. This keeps Clio lightweight on your Termux/low RAM device, and it also means you always get the latest version of a module. Once downloaded, they work **offline-first**, just like Clio itself!

## Setup Wizards vs. Automation Modules: A Quick Look

It's easy to confuse Automation Modules with Setup Wizards because both help you get things done. However, their purpose and how you use them are different. Let's clarify:

| Feature           | Setup Wizards                                    | Automation Modules                                     |
| :---------------- | :----------------------------------------------- | :----------------------------------------------------- |
| **Purpose**       | Install and configure environments (e.g., Vim, Git, Termux). | Perform repeatable tasks (e.g., backup, check disk space). |
| **When to use**   | Once or occasionally, for initial setup or major changes. | Whenever you need to do a specific task, often repeatedly. |
| **How to browse** | `>> setup`                                       | `>> modules`                                           |
| **Clio Label**    | `[SETUP WIZARD]`                                 | `[AUTOMATION]`                                         |
| **Example**       | `>> setup git` (installs Git)                    | `>> backup project_folder` (backs up a folder)         |

**Key takeaway:** Setup Wizards get your environment ready; Automation Modules help you *work* within that environment.

## Browsing Available Modules

Clio makes it easy to see what modules are available from the CLIPilot registry.

### 1. Listing Automation Modules

To see only the automation modules, use the `modules` command:

```bash
>> modules
```

Clio will connect to the CLIPilot registry (if you're online) and show you a list of available modules, each with a description and its unique ID. You'll see entries like:

```
[AUTOMATION] check_disk_space - Checks available disk space on your device.
   -> download check_disk_space
   -> clio-run-module check_disk_space

[AUTOMATION] backup_folder - Creates a compressed backup of a specified directory.
   -> download backup_folder
   -> clio-run-module backup_folder <folder_path>

[AUTOMATION] install_python_package - Installs a Python package using pip.
   -> download install_python_package
   -> clio-run-module install_python_package <package_name>
```

Notice how each entry clearly tells you:
*   Its purpose.
*   The command to `download` it.
*   The command to `clio-run-module` it once downloaded, often with hints for arguments (like `<folder_path>`).

### 2. Listing Everything with `catalog`

If you want to see both **Setup Wizards** and **Automation Modules** in one go, use the `catalog` command:

```bash
>> catalog
```

This command provides a comprehensive list, clearly separating the entries with their respective `[SETUP WIZARD]` or `[AUTOMATION]` labels. It's great for exploring everything Clio offers.

## Downloading Automation Modules

Before you can run a module, you need to download it from the CLIPilot registry. This makes it available on your device, even when you're offline.

### 1. Downloading a Single Module

If you know the ID of the module you want, you can download it directly. For example, to get the module for checking disk space:

```bash
>> download check_disk_space
```

Clio will fetch that specific module and confirm its download. It's now ready to be used!

### 2. Bulk Syncing Modules

For a more general approach, you can use the `sync` command:

```bash
>> sync
```

This command downloads a set of essential and commonly used modules. This is particularly useful for beginners to get a good starting set without picking them one by one.

**Important for Termux/low RAM users:** If you're using Clio on a **Lite profile** (common on Termux or devices with low RAM), `sync` will only download a curated list of essential modules to keep your storage and resource usage minimal.

If you want *all* available modules from the registry, even on a Lite profile, you can use:

```bash
>> sync full
```

This will download the complete catalog of modules, so be mindful of your storage space.

## Running Automation Modules

Once a module is downloaded, you can run it using the `clio-run-module` command, followed by the module's ID.

### 1. Running a Module Directly

Let's say you downloaded `check_disk_space`. To run it:

```bash
>> clio-run-module check_disk_space
```

Clio will then execute the commands defined within that module. If the module involves shell commands, Clio will always **confirm before running** them, showing you what it's about to do. This is a crucial safety feature!

Some modules might require additional information (arguments). For example, the `backup_folder` module needs to know *which* folder to backup:

```bash
>> clio-run-module backup_folder /data/data/com.termux/files/home/my_project
```

Or, if you saw `clio-run-module <module_id> setup` in the `modules` output, it means that particular module has a `setup` sub-command or mode:

```bash
>> clio-run-module install_python_package setup
```
(This might, for example, prompt you for a list of packages to install sequentially).

### 2. Using Natural Language to Run Modules

This is where Clio truly shines! Instead of remembering module IDs or specific commands, you can just tell Clio what you want to do in natural language – even in Nigerian Pidgin!

If Clio understands your request and finds a matching automation module, it will propose that module.

**Examples:**

*   **English:**
    ```bash
    >> check my storage
    ```
    Clio might respond:
    ```
    SHELL COMMAND / AUTOMATION MODULE / SETUP WIZARD
    📦 [AUTOMATION MODULE] check_disk_space - Checks available disk space on your device.
    Do you want to run 'clio-run-module check_disk_space'? (y/n)
    ```

*   **Nigerian Pidgin:**
    ```bash
    >> abeg wetin dey my disk space?
    ```
    Clio will still understand and suggest:
    ```
    SHELL COMMAND / AUTOMATION MODULE / SETUP WIZARD
    📦 [AUTOMATION MODULE] check_disk_space - Checks available disk space on your device.
    Do you want to run 'clio-run-module check_disk_space'? (y/n)
    ```

*   **English:**
    ```bash
    >> I want to backup my notes folder
    ```
    Clio might suggest:
    ```
    SHELL COMMAND / AUTOMATION MODULE / SETUP WIZARD
    📦 [AUTOMATION MODULE] backup_folder - Creates a compressed backup of a specified directory.
    Do you want to run 'clio-run-module backup_folder <folder_path>'? (y/n)
    ```
    Clio will then likely prompt you for the specific folder path if you say 'y'.

Always remember to confirm with `y` when Clio shows you a `Do you want to run...?` prompt. This gives you control over what commands are executed on your system. The header `📦 [AUTOMATION MODULE]` will clearly show you that Clio has matched your request to an automation module.

## Try It Now! Practice Steps

Let's get practical and try out some automation!

1.  **Browse Modules:**
    ```bash
    >> modules
    ```
    Look through the list. Find a module that sounds useful to you, maybe `check_disk_space` or `backup_folder`. Note down its ID.

2.  **Download a Module:**
    Let's download `check_disk_space`.
    ```bash
    >> download check_disk_space
    ```
    Clio should confirm the download.

3.  **Run the Module Directly:**
    Now, run the module you just downloaded:
    ```bash
    >> clio-run-module check_disk_space
    ```
    Clio will show you what commands it's about to run (e.g., `df -h`). Type `y` and press Enter to confirm. See your disk space!

4.  **Try Natural Language:**
    Now, ask Clio to do the same thing using natural language:
    ```bash
    >> wetin dey my storage?
    ```
    or
    ```bash
    >> check my disk
    ```
    Clio should propose the `check_disk_space` module. Confirm with `y` and see it run again!

5.  **Explore `sync full` (Optional):**
    If you have enough storage and want to see the full range of modules, try:
    ```bash
    >> sync full
    ```
    This might take a moment depending on your internet connection. After it's done, run `modules` again to see the expanded list.

## Common Mistakes and Troubleshooting

*   **"Module not found" error:** This usually means you haven't downloaded the module yet. Use `>> download <module_id>` or `>> sync` first.
*   **"Command not found: clio-run-module":** Ensure Clio is correctly installed and its binaries are in your system's PATH. If you just installed Clio, you might need to restart your Termux session.
*   **Module runs but gives an error:** The module might require specific arguments (like a folder path). Check the `modules` output for hints on how to run it, or try asking Clio for help with the module's name. Example: `>> help backup_folder`.
*   **`sync` doesn't download many modules:** Remember, if you're on a Lite profile, `sync` is optimized to download only essentials. Use `>> sync full` if you need everything.
*   **Permission issues:** Some modules might try to access areas of your Android phone that Termux doesn't have permission for by default. Ensure Termux has storage permissions.

## Your Automation Modules Checklist

By now, you should be comfortable with:

*   [ ] Understanding what Automation Modules are and why they are useful.
*   [ ] Knowing the difference between Setup Wizards and Automation Modules.
*   [ ] Browsing available modules using `>> modules` and `>> catalog`.
*   [ ] Downloading individual modules with `>> download <id>`.
*   [ ] Bulk syncing modules with `>> sync` and `>> sync full`.
*   [ ] Running downloaded modules using `>> clio-run-module <id>`.
*   [ ] Using natural language (including Pidgin!) to trigger modules.
*   [ ] Confirming shell commands before they run for safety.

Well done, my friend! You've unlocked a powerful way to make your daily tasks much easier with Clio. In our next lesson, we'll dive deeper into managing your modules and other advanced tips. Keep practicing!

lessons.title: Lesson 5 — Offline Mode & Sync
lessons.description: Understand lite profile, layers, and when you need internet.
lessons.content:
# Lesson 5 — Offline Mode & Sync

Welcome back, champions! For many of us using our Android phones with Termux, data can be expensive, and internet connections might not always be reliable. Imagine you're in a place with no Wi-Fi or your data bundle just finished. Will your trusty Clio assistant still work?

The answer is a resounding **YES, for most things!** This lesson will show you how Clio is built to be your reliable helper, even when offline. We're going to dive into how Clio works its magic without the internet, what a "lite profile" means for your phone, and when you absolutely need to get online.

## Clio: Your Offline-First Assistant

Clio is designed from the ground up to be **offline-first**. This means that its primary goal is to help you *without* needing an internet connection. Most of the common questions you'll ask and the basic commands you'll use are processed right on your device, instantly.

Think of it like having a super-smart friend who has memorized a whole library of commands and solutions. They don't need to call anyone or look anything up online for the things they already know. That's Clio for you!

## Clio's Intelligence Layers: How It Finds Answers

When you type a question into Clio, it doesn't just immediately search the internet. Instead, it uses a clever system of "layers" to find the best answer, starting with what's fastest and closest to you. It only moves to the next layer if it can't find a good match in the current one.

Here’s how Clio searches for answers:

### 1. Phrase & Catalog (Built-in & Instant)
This is Clio's first stop and its fastest layer. It contains the most common commands, basic Linux utilities, and frequently asked questions. These are built directly into the Clio binary (the app itself) when you install it. This means they are *always* available, no internet needed, no extra downloads.

*   **Example (Pidgin):** `>> abeg, how I fit check my current directory?` (Please, how can I check my current directory?)
*   **Example (English):** `>> how to list files`
*   **Availability:** Always offline.

### 2. Man Pages (Local Manuals)
If the phrase and catalog layer doesn't find a perfect match, Clio then checks your system's local "man pages" (manual pages). These are detailed documentation files for various commands that are usually installed with your operating system (like Termux). Clio searches through these for relevant information.

**Important Note:** On a **lite profile** (which is common for Termux/low RAM devices), this layer is often skipped to save memory and processing power. This helps Clio stay super fast and light on your phone!

*   **Example:** `>> explain ls command`
*   **Availability:** Offline (if man pages are installed and not on lite profile).

### 3. Automation Modules (Cached & Smart)
If Clio still hasn't found what you need, it looks at its collection of **automation modules**. These modules are like mini-programs or scripts that Clio uses to perform more complex or repeatable tasks. When you download a module (either individually with `download <id>` or through `sync`), it gets stored in Clio's local database (`~/.clio/clio.db`).

Once a module is downloaded and cached, Clio can use it **offline**! This is super powerful for tasks like setting up Git, configuring Vim, or even interacting with a local database, all without needing to be online *after* the initial download.

*   **Example:** `>> I wan setup git for my Termux` (I want to set up Git for my Termux)
    *   *Initial download of the Git setup module would require internet, but subsequent runs of related tasks can be offline.*
*   **Example:** `>> run python script my_script.py`
*   **Availability:** Offline, *after* initial download/sync.

### 4. Remote Search (CLIPilot API)
This is Clio's last resort and the *only* layer that requires an active internet connection. If all local layers fail to find a suitable answer, Clio will then reach out to the CLIPilot registry online. This is where it can discover new modules, get answers to very obscure questions, or fetch the latest information.

*   **Example:** `>> how to use a niche command I've never heard of`
*   **Availability:** **Online only.**

Here’s a quick summary of Clio's layers:

| Layer                     | Description                                            | Internet Needed?  | Lite Profile Impact                                |
| :------------------------ | :----------------------------------------------------- | :---------------- | :------------------------------------------------- |
| **1. Phrase & Catalog**   | Core commands, basic Linux, common questions (built-in) | No                | Always available                                   |
| **2. Man Pages**          | System manual pages for commands                       | No                | **Often skipped** to save memory                   |
| **3. Automation Modules** | Repeatable tasks, setup wizards                        | Yes (for download)| Cached modules work offline after initial download |
| **4. Remote Search**      | CLIPilot registry for new info, obscure questions      | Yes               | Only used if local search fails                    |

## Understanding the Lite Profile (Especially for Termux)

Many Nigerian students use Termux on Android phones, which often have limited RAM (memory). Clio is super smart about this! By default, it often runs in a **lite profile** to ensure it's fast and doesn't hog your phone's resources.

What does "lite profile" mean for you?

*   **Smaller Memory Footprint:** Clio uses less RAM, keeping your phone smooth.
*   **Optimized Storage:** `sync` downloads only essential modules, saving space on your device.
*   **Man Pages Skipped:** As mentioned, the man page layer might be skipped to further optimize performance.

You can see (and even change, though `auto` is usually best) Clio's profile settings in its configuration file at `~/.clio/config.yaml`.

```yaml
profile: auto   # auto | lite | full. 'auto' detects your system's capabilities.
remote_search: auto # auto | always | never. 'auto' uses remote search if local fails.
```

When you run the `sync` command, Clio in lite profile will only fetch the most critical and frequently used modules. If you ever want *all* available modules to be cached locally (perhaps you have a lot of storage and a strong internet connection), you can use `sync full`.

```bash
>> sync
```
*This downloads essential modules for offline use.*

```bash
>> sync full
```
*This downloads ALL available modules from the CLIPilot registry for comprehensive offline use.*

**Remember:** Even with `sync full`, new modules are constantly being added to the CLIPilot registry. If you ask for something very new or specific that hasn't been synced yet, Clio might still need to go online.

## When You Absolutely Need Internet (No Way Around It!)

While Clio is awesome offline, there are a few specific situations where an internet connection is *required*. These are mostly for initial setup or when you're fetching something completely new.

1.  **Installing Clio Itself:** The very first time you get Clio onto your Termux, you need to be online to run the `curl -fsSL https://clipilot.themobileprof.com/clio | sh` command. This downloads and installs the core Clio binary.
2.  **First-Time Module Download:**
    *   When you use `download <id>` for a specific module.
    *   When you run `sync` or `sync full` to populate your local module cache.
    *   When you use a **setup wizard** (e.g., `setup git`, `setup vim`, `setup termux`) for the first time. The setup wizard itself is an automation module that needs to be downloaded before it can run.
3.  **Remote Search:** As discussed, if Clio cannot find an answer within its local layers (phrase/catalog, man pages, cached modules), it will attempt a remote search on the CLIPilot registry. This requires internet.

## Local Storage: Where Clio Keeps Its Brain

To function so brilliantly offline, Clio needs to store some information on your device. It keeps two important files in a hidden directory called `.clio` in your home folder (`~`).

*   **`~/.clio/clio.db`:** This is Clio's local database. It's where all your downloaded automation modules are cached. This database is what allows Clio to run complex tasks and setup wizards even when you're offline, after the initial download.
*   **`~/.clio/config.yaml`:** This is Clio's configuration file. It stores settings like your profile (auto, lite, full) and remote search preferences. You generally don't need to edit this, but it's good to know where it lives.

Knowing about these files helps you understand how Clio maintains its offline capabilities.

## Try It Now: Experience Offline Power!

Let's put Clio's offline capabilities to the test!

1.  **Make sure you have some modules synced.** If you haven't run `sync` recently, do it now while you're online:
    ```bash
    >> sync
    ```
    *Wait for it to complete.*
2.  **Turn off your Wi-Fi and mobile data.** Go to your phone's settings and completely disable your internet connection.
3.  **Open Termux and launch Clio.**
4.  **Ask Clio some common questions that should work offline:**
    *   `>> wetin be my current directory?` (What is my current directory?)
    *   `>> how to list files in a folder`
    *   `>> create a new folder named 'my_projects'`
    *   `>> I wan setup git` (If you've synced the git module, this should start offline. Clio might confirm it's an AUTOMATION MODULE.)
    *   `>> show me my ip address`
    *   `>> exit`
    *   *Notice how quickly Clio responds! These answers come from its local brain.*
5.  **Now, try to ask for something very specific or new that you likely haven't synced.**
    *   `>> what is the latest version of Rust programming language?`
    *   *You might get a message indicating Clio is trying a remote search, or it might say it can't find an answer locally.*
6.  **Turn your internet back on.**
7.  **Relaunch Clio and try running `sync` again.**
    ```bash
    >> sync
    ```
    *This will check for any new essential modules or updates to existing ones.*
8.  **Try the Rust question again.** Now that you're online, Clio should be able to perform a remote search and give you an answer (if available in the CLIPilot registry).

This exercise helps you feel the difference and understand when Clio needs the internet and when it doesn't.

## Common Mistakes & Troubleshooting

*   **"My module isn't working offline!"**
    *   **Solution:** Did you `sync` or `download` it while online *before* going offline? Modules must be cached locally first. Run `sync` or `download <id>` when you have internet.
*   **"Clio is slow sometimes."**
    *   **Solution:** This usually happens when Clio can't find a local match and tries a "remote search" (Layer 4). If your internet is slow or unstable, this can cause a delay. Check your internet connection. If you want to prevent remote searches, you could theoretically change `remote_search: auto` to `remote_search: never` in `~/.clio/config.yaml`, but this means Clio won't find anything new online.

## Your Offline Clio Checklist

You've learned a lot about how Clio keeps working for you, even when the network is down. Here's a quick checklist to recap the key points:

*   **Offline-First:** Clio prioritizes local answers for speed and reliability.
*   **Intelligence Layers:** Clio searches in stages: built-in phrases/catalog, then local man pages (often skipped on lite), then cached modules, and finally remote search (online).
*   **Lite Profile:** On Termux/low RAM, Clio uses less memory and only syncs essential modules by default.
*   **`sync` vs. `sync full`:** `sync` gets essentials; `sync full` gets everything (requires more storage and initial internet).
*   **Internet Needed For:** Initial installation, first-time module downloads/syncs, and remote searches.
*   **Local Storage:** Clio keeps its cached modules in `~/.clio/clio.db` and configuration in `~/.clio/config.yaml`.

You are now well-equipped to understand and leverage Clio's powerful offline capabilities, making it an indispensable tool for your learning journey on Termux! Next, we'll explore how to get more specific help with Clio's `help` command and how to exit gracefully.

lessons.title: Lesson 6 — Termux on Android
lessons.description: Phone-specific tips: install, clio-run-module, storage, and 32-bit devices.
lessons.content:
# Lesson 6 — Termux on Android

Welcome to Lesson 6! For many of us, especially students in Nigeria, our smartphone is our primary computer. Termux on Android transforms your phone into a powerful Linux environment, and Clio is built from the ground up to be your best friend in this setup. This lesson will guide you through getting the most out of Clio on your Android phone, covering installation, unique Termux features, and tips for smooth operation.

By the end of this lesson, you'll be confident using Clio to manage your phone-based coding projects, from setting up development tools to handling files, all with natural language.

## Installing Clio on Termux

Getting Clio up and running on Termux is straightforward. The process ensures you have all the necessary components for a seamless experience.

First, ensure Termux has `curl`, a command-line tool for transferring data, which is needed to fetch the Clio installer.

```bash
pkg install curl
```

Once `curl` is installed, you can proceed with the Clio installation. This command downloads the installer script from the CLIPilot registry and executes it.

```bash
curl -fsSL https://clipilot.themobileprof.com/clio | sh
```

**What happens during installation?**
This script intelligently detects your device's architecture (e.g., `arm64` for most modern phones or `arm` for older ones, more on this below) and downloads the correct Clio binary. It then places two important executables in your Termux `$PREFIX/bin/` directory:
*   `clio`: This is the main Clio assistant you interact with.
*   `clio-run-module`: This is a special helper script crucial for running automation modules on Termux, which we'll discuss next.

You'll know the installation is successful when you see a confirmation message, and you can then type `clio` to start your assistant.

## Understanding `clio-run-module`: Why It's Essential on Android

Clio's powerful automation modules are designed to perform repeatable tasks, from setting up development environments to managing projects. However, Termux (and Android in general) has specific security restrictions. For example, Android blocks certain low-level system calls (syscalls) that Go binaries (like Clio itself) might try to use directly.

To work around these restrictions and ensure modules run reliably, Clio uses a clever approach: **all module workflows on Termux are executed via `clio-run-module`, which is a simple bash script.**

**How it works:**
When Clio needs to run an automation module, instead of executing it directly, it generates a temporary bash script containing the module's logic and then invokes `clio-run-module` to execute *that* bash script. This allows the module to operate within Termux's environment without hitting Android's syscall limitations for Go programs.

**Key takeaway:** You will rarely (if ever) need to directly use `clio-run-module` yourself. Clio handles this automatically in the background when you ask it to perform an automation task or run a setup wizard.

**Important:** Never try to manually paste module YAML code or scripts you find online directly into your terminal. Let Clio manage the download and execution of modules from the trusted CLIPilot registry. This ensures security and proper execution via `clio-run-module`.

**Example:**
When you ask Clio to set up Termux, it might internally use `clio-run-module`:

```
>> setup termux
```
Clio will then download the `termux_setup` module from the CLIPilot registry and execute it safely using `clio-run-module`. You'll see the setup wizard guide you through the process.

## Your First Steps: The Termux Setup Wizard

After installing Clio, there's a recommended sequence of actions to get your Termux environment fully ready for development.

1.  **Install Clio:** (Done!)
    ```bash
    curl -fsSL https://clipilot.themobileprof.com/clio | sh
    ```
2.  **Run Clio:** Type `clio` at your Termux prompt to launch the assistant.
    ```bash
    clio
    ```
    You'll see the `>>` prompt.
3.  **Complete the `setup termux` wizard:** This is crucial for optimizing Clio for your Termux environment. It performs essential configurations like granting storage access and installing common utilities.
    ```
    >> setup termux
    ```
    Alternatively, if you just type `setup`, Clio will likely recommend `setup termux` if it detects you're on Termux. Follow the on-screen prompts.
4.  **Synchronize Clio's knowledge:** Once you have internet access (which you likely used for installation), run `sync`. This downloads the latest phrase and catalog definitions, ensuring Clio has up-to-date information and can work more effectively offline.
    ```
    >> sync
    ```
    If you're ever online for a longer period, `sync full` downloads even more data, including `man` pages for offline reference.
5.  **Start asking questions!** Your Clio is now ready to assist you.

## Optimizing for Your Phone: 32-bit Devices & Lite Profile

Clio is designed to be efficient, especially for the diverse range of Android devices used by students.

### 32-bit vs. 64-bit Phones
Many older or more affordable Android phones use a 32-bit architecture (often `armv7l`). Modern phones typically use 64-bit (`armv8l` or `aarch64`). The good news is you don't need to worry about this!

The Clio install script (`curl -fsSL ... | sh`) automatically detects your phone's architecture. If it finds `armv7l`, it downloads the `arm` (32-bit) binary. If it finds `armv8l` or `aarch64`, it downloads the `arm64` (64-bit) binary. This ensures you always get the correct, optimized version of Clio for your specific device.

### Lite Profile for Low-RAM Devices
Clio understands that many Termux users are on devices with limited RAM. It automatically operates in a "Lite profile" on Termux to conserve resources.

This means Clio prioritizes its core functions:
*   **Phrase Matching:** Understanding your natural language queries.
*   **Catalog Lookups:** Quickly finding relevant commands and modules from its local knowledge base.

Other, more resource-intensive layers, like full `man` page lookups (which can be large), extensive module loading, or remote searches (which require network and more processing), are accessed on demand or when more resources are available. This ensures Clio remains responsive even on phones with low RAM, making your experience smooth and efficient.

## Accessing Your Files: Storage on Termux

One of the most common tasks on a phone is managing files. Termux, by default, isolates its file system from the rest of your Android storage for security reasons. This means you can't directly access your phone's `Downloads` folder or SD card from Termux without explicit permission.

The `setup termux` wizard is crucial here because it automatically runs `termux-setup-storage`. This command prompts your Android system to grant Termux permission to access shared storage.

Once `termux-setup-storage` has been run and you've granted permission, Termux creates a special directory at `~/storage/`. Inside `~/storage/`, you'll find symlinks (shortcuts) to common Android directories:

| Link Path          | Corresponds to...                          |
| :----------------- | :----------------------------------------- |
| `~/storage/dcim`   | Your camera photos/videos                  |
| `~/storage/downloads`| Your phone's Downloads folder              |
| `~/storage/external-1`| Your SD card (if present)                  |
| `~/storage/music`  | Your music folder                          |
| `~/storage/pictures`| Your pictures folder                       |
| `~/storage/shared` | The root of your internal shared storage   |

**Example:**
Now you can ask Clio to interact with these folders:

```
>> wetin dey inside downloads folder
>> abeg delete all files for ~/storage/downloads wey dey old pass 30 days
>> move my-project.zip from ~/storage/shared to current folder
```

This integration makes it much easier to work with files downloaded from browsers, shared via messaging apps, or stored on an SD card, directly from your Termux environment with Clio's help.

## Speak Your Language: Pidgin on Clio

Clio understands Nigerian Pidgin, making it incredibly intuitive for students. You don't need to translate your thoughts into formal English commands; just tell Clio what you want to do in a way that feels natural. This is particularly empowering when you're learning to code and might not yet be fluent in technical English jargon.

**Examples of Pidgin commands on your phone:**

```
>> abeg show me wetin dey my working directory
SHELL COMMAND: ls -F
Confirm before run? (y/n): y
```

```
>> wetin dey inside downloads folder
SHELL COMMAND: ls -F ~/storage/downloads
Confirm before run? (y/n): y
```

```
>> abeg check my phone storage space
SHELL COMMAND: df -h
Confirm before run? (y/n): y
```

```
>> I wan install python for this Termux
SETUP WIZARD: setup devtools python
Confirm before run? (y/n): y
```
Clio will interpret "I wan install python" as a request to run the Python setup wizard within the `devtools` category, simplifying complex installations.

## Safety First: Confirm Before Running Commands

As a beginner, it's natural to be cautious when running commands in the terminal, especially on your phone. Clio has a built-in safety feature: it will **always ask for your confirmation before executing any shell command, automation module, or setup wizard.**

When Clio proposes a solution, it will clearly show you:
1.  **The proposed command or action.**
2.  **A header indicating the type of action:**
    *   `SHELL COMMAND:` for direct terminal commands.
    *   `AUTOMATION MODULE:` for repeatable tasks from the CLIPilot registry.
    *   `SETUP WIZARD:` for guided installation and configuration processes.
3.  **A confirmation prompt:** `Confirm before run? (y/n):`

This gives you a chance to review what Clio intends to do. If you're unsure, you can type `n` to cancel and rephrase your query, or ask Clio to explain the command (e.g., "wetin `ls -F` dey do?"). This feature is invaluable for learning and preventing accidental changes to your system.

## Troubleshooting Common Termux Issues

Even with Clio's help, you might encounter a few bumps. Here's a quick guide to common Termux-specific issues:

| Problem                                | Possible Cause & Fix

lessons.title: Lesson 7 — Practice & Next Steps
lessons.description: Capstone exercises, command reference, and where to get help.
lessons.content:
# Lesson 7 — Practice & Next Steps

**Lesson summary:** Capstone exercises, command reference, and where to get help.
**Target duration:** 12 minutes

Weldone, my friend! You've journeyed through the world of Clio, your intelligent terminal assistant. From installation to understanding its core features, you've gained powerful skills that will transform how you interact with your Termux or Linux terminal. No more struggling with complex commands or endless Googling – Clio is here to simplify your digital life, right from your Android phone.

This final lesson is your chance to put everything you've learned into practice. We'll tackle some real-world challenges, review Clio's essential commands, and make sure you know where to go next on your learning adventure. Get ready to become a true terminal master with Clio by your side!

## Capstone Challenges: Show Your Clio Skills!

It's time for the ultimate test! These challenges are designed to help you consolidate your knowledge and build confidence. Try to complete them using only Clio, without resorting to traditional search engines for the raw shell commands. Remember, Clio is your guide!

For each challenge, type your natural language request at the `>>` prompt and follow Clio's instructions. Pay attention to the match headers (SHELL COMMAND, AUTOMATION MODULE, SETUP WIZARD) and always confirm before Clio runs any command.

### Challenge 1: Files — "Show hidden files with details in this folder"

On a phone, managing files can sometimes be tricky, especially when you need to see everything that's there. Hidden files often store important configuration or system data, and knowing how to view them is a fundamental skill.

**Your task:** Ask Clio to display all files, including hidden ones, along with their detailed information (like permissions, size, and modification date) in your current directory.

```
>> show hidden files with details in this folder
```

**What to expect:**
Clio should identify this as a `SHELL COMMAND`. It will then show you the actual command it plans to execute (likely something similar to `ls -la`). Review the command, and if it looks correct, type `y` (or `yes`) to confirm and see the output. This is a great way to learn the underlying Linux commands too!

### Challenge 2: Space — "Check how much disk space is left"

Storage is always a concern on mobile devices. Knowing how much space you have remaining is crucial for managing your apps, downloads, and project files. Clio makes this check super easy.

**Your task:** Ask Clio to tell you how much free disk space is available on your device. You can even try this in Nigerian Pidgin!

```
>> check how much disk space is left
```

Or, if you're feeling adventurous:

```
>> wetin remain for my storage?
```

**What to expect:**
Clio will likely find a `SHELL COMMAND` that uses tools like `df -h`. Confirm the command, and Clio will present you with a summary of your disk usage, showing total, used, and available space in a human-readable format.

### Challenge 3: Search — "Find files larger than 50 megabytes"

Large files can quickly eat up your precious storage. Being able to quickly locate these space hogs is a powerful feature for maintaining a tidy system.

**Your task:** Ask Clio to find all files in your current directory (and its subdirectories) that are larger than 50 megabytes.

```
>> find files larger than 50 megabytes
```

**What to expect:**
Clio will propose a `SHELL COMMAND` (likely involving `find` with size parameters). Confirm it, and you'll see a list of files that meet your criteria. This can be incredibly useful for identifying and managing large media files or old downloads.

### Challenge 4: Setup — Run one setup wizard you have not tried yet

Remember those amazing setup wizards that configure complex tools for you? If you haven't explored them all, now's your chance! These wizards automate installations and configurations that would normally take a lot of manual steps and potential errors.

**Available Setup Wizards:**
*   `termux`
*   `vim`
*   `git`
*   `devtools`
*   `database`

**Your task:** Choose one setup wizard from the list above that you haven't explored yet, and run it. For example, if you're interested in version control, try the `git` wizard. If you want to set up a powerful text editor, try `vim`.

```
>> setup git
```

Or:

```
>> setup vim
```

**What to expect:**
Clio will identify this as a `SETUP WIZARD`. Confirm, and the wizard will guide you through an interactive process, asking questions and performing necessary installations and configurations. This is a fantastic way to get complex tools ready for use without the headache.

### Challenge 5: Module — Browse `modules`, download one, and read its description

Automation modules are Clio's secret weapon for repeatable, complex tasks. They live in the CLIPilot registry and are not bundled with Clio itself, meaning you can always get the latest and greatest tools.

**Your task:**
1.  List the available automation modules.
2.  Choose one that sounds interesting.
3.  Download it.
4.  Understand what it does by reading its description.

```
>> modules
```

**What to expect for step 1:**
Clio will display a list of available `AUTOMATION MODULES` from the CLIPilot registry. Each module will have an `ID` and a brief description. Note down the `ID` of a module you'd like to try.

**Your task for step 2 & 3:** Now, use the `download` command with the ID you noted. Let's say you picked a module with `ID: 123`.

```
>> download 123
```

**What to expect for step 2 & 3:** Clio will confirm the download. Once downloaded, the module is ready to be used!

**Your task for step 4:** When you initially listed the modules using `>> modules` or `>> catalog`, you saw a brief description for each. That's how you "read its description" before deciding to download. After downloading, you can often trigger the module's functionality using natural language related to its purpose. For example, if you downloaded a "backup" module, you might then type `>> backup my important files`. The module itself might then provide further interactive prompts or help.

**Congratulations!** You've navigated Clio's core functionalities. You're now equipped to handle a wide range of tasks with ease.

## Quick Command Reference

As you continue your journey, you might sometimes forget the exact command for a specific Clio action. This quick reference table is your cheat sheet!

| Goal (What you want to do)                  | Clio Command (How to do it)        | Description
